services.name: S3
services.tagline: Object storage built to retrieve any amount of data
services.description: Scalable object storage with 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability. Store and retrieve any amount of data from anywhere on the web.
keyPoints: Storage classes: Standard, IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier, Glacier Deep Archive, Intelligent-Tiering
Bucket policies and ACLs for access control
Versioning: keep multiple variants of an object
Lifecycle rules: automatically transition or expire objects
Event notifications: trigger Lambda, SQS, SNS on object changes
examTips: S3 Standard: 99.99% availability, 11 nines durability
S3 IA: lower cost but retrieval fee — use for infrequent access
Glacier: minutes to hours retrieval. Deep Archive: 12-48 hours
S3 Transfer Acceleration uses CloudFront edge locations for fast uploads
Server-side encryption: SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C

quiz.q: What is the durability of S3 Standard?
quiz.explanation: S3 is designed for 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability, meaning statistically you'd lose one object per 10,000 years across 10 million objects.

quiz.q: Which S3 storage class automatically moves objects between access tiers?
quiz.explanation: S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between frequent and infrequent access tiers based on usage patterns, with no retrieval fees.

services.name: EBS
services.description: High-performance block storage designed for use with EC2. Like a virtual hard drive that persists independently of the instance lifecycle.
keyPoints: Volume types: gp3 (general), io2 (high IOPS), st1 (throughput HDD), sc1 (cold HDD)
Snapshots: point-in-time backups stored in S3
Encryption: AES-256, integrates with KMS
Multi-attach: io2 volumes can attach to multiple instances (same AZ)
Volumes are AZ-specific — use snapshots to move across AZs
examTips: gp3: 3000 IOPS baseline, up to 16000. Better price-performance than gp2
io2 Block Express: up to 256,000 IOPS for mission-critical databases
EBS volumes persist after instance termination (unless DeleteOnTermination=true)
Snapshots are incremental — only changed blocks are stored
RAID 0 for performance, RAID 1 for redundancy (not recommended — use Multi-AZ)

quiz.q: Which EBS volume type provides the highest IOPS for databases?
quiz.explanation: io2 (and io2 Block Express) provides the highest IOPS — up to 256,000 IOPS — designed for critical databases like Oracle and SAP HANA.

quiz.q: How can you move an EBS volume to a different Availability Zone?
options: Create a snapshot and restore in the new AZ
quiz.explanation: EBS volumes are AZ-locked. To move to another AZ, create a snapshot (stored in S3) and create a new volume from that snapshot in the target AZ.

services.name: EFS
services.tagline: Scalable file storage for EC2 and containers
services.description: Fully managed, elastic NFS file system. Multiple instances can access the same file system concurrently, making it ideal for shared workloads.
keyPoints: NFS v4.1 protocol — mount from multiple EC2 instances simultaneously
Automatically grows and shrinks as you add/remove files
Storage classes: Standard and Infrequent Access (IA)
Performance modes: General Purpose and Max I/O
Throughput modes: Bursting, Provisioned, Elastic
examTips: EFS is regional — accessible from multiple AZs (unlike EBS which is single-AZ)
EFS is more expensive than EBS per GB but offers shared access
Use lifecycle policies to move files to IA after N days (cost savings)
EFS for Linux only — use FSx for Windows workloads
Lambda can mount EFS for shared persistent storage between invocations

quiz.explanation: EFS uses the NFS v4.1 protocol. For SMB/CIFS (Windows), use Amazon FSx for Windows File Server.

services.name: S3 Glacier
services.description: Secure, durable, and extremely low-cost cloud storage for data archiving and long-term backup. Retrieval times from minutes to hours.
keyPoints: Three tiers: Glacier Instant, Glacier Flexible, Glacier Deep Archive
Glacier Instant: millisecond retrieval, quarterly access pattern
Glacier Flexible: 1-5 min (Expedited), 3-5 hr (Standard), 5-12 hr (Bulk)
Deep Archive: cheapest storage ($0.00099/GB/mo), 12-48 hour retrieval
Vault Lock: WORM compliance with policy-based locking
examTips: Deep Archive: minimum 180-day storage commitment
Glacier Flexible: minimum 90-day storage commitment
Vault Lock cannot be changed after lock — ensures compliance
Use S3 Lifecycle rules to automatically move to Glacier
Expedited retrieval: 1-5 minutes but higher cost per GB

quiz.q: What is the minimum storage duration for S3 Glacier Deep Archive?
quiz.explanation: S3 Glacier Deep Archive has a minimum storage duration of 180 days. You are charged for 180 days even if you delete earlier.

services.name: FSx
services.description: Launch and run popular file systems in the cloud. Options include FSx for Windows File Server, FSx for Lustre, FSx for NetApp ONTAP, and FSx for OpenZFS.
keyPoints: FSx for Windows: SMB protocol, Active Directory integration, NTFS
FSx for Lustre: high-performance computing, ML training, video processing
FSx for NetApp ONTAP: multi-protocol (NFS, SMB, iSCSI), snapshots, clones
FSx for OpenZFS: high-performance NFS with snapshots and compression
Lustre can be linked to S3 for transparent data access
examTips: Windows workloads needing shared file storage → FSx for Windows (not EFS!)
HPC/ML needing fast parallel file access → FSx for Lustre
Lustre scratch: temporary, high performance. Persistent: long-term with replication
FSx for Windows supports DFS namespaces for file share grouping
All FSx types are deployed in a VPC with security group access

quiz.q: Which FSx type should you choose for Windows-based workloads needing Active Directory integration?
quiz.explanation: FSx for Windows File Server provides native SMB protocol support and Active Directory integration for Windows workloads.

services.name: Storage Gateway
services.description: Hybrid cloud storage service that gives on-premises applications access to virtually unlimited cloud storage. Bridges on-prem and AWS.
keyPoints: File Gateway: NFS/SMB interface to S3 objects
Volume Gateway: iSCSI block storage backed by S3 (Stored or Cached)
Tape Gateway: virtual tape library backed by S3/Glacier
Deployed as VM on-premises or as hardware appliance
Local cache for low-latency access to frequently used data
examTips: File Gateway: files stored as S3 objects — full S3 feature access
Cached Volumes: primary data in S3, hot data cached locally
Stored Volumes: primary data on-prem, async backup to S3
Tape Gateway replaces physical tape backup infrastructure
All data transfer encrypted in transit (SSL) and at rest

quiz.q: Which Storage Gateway type provides block storage with data primarily stored in S3?
quiz.explanation: Volume Gateway in Cached mode stores primary data in S3 with frequently accessed data cached locally, providing low-latency iSCSI access.
